text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures with fluctuating control; previous traumatic head injury; persistent cognitive slowing; osteoporosis; prior alcohol dependence (in remission); lives in supported accommodation; current smoker; keeps an assistance dog in training.

Current medication:  
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily  
- Carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily  
- Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening  
- Thiamine, Vitamin B compound, Colecalciferol, Calcium supplement, Atorvastatin, Lansoprazole, Bisoprolol, Ramipril, Carbocisteine, Emollient cream

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his key worker and his assistance dog in training. He reports the dog is helping with day-to-day structure and confidence outdoors. Appetite is stable and weight has been steady over the past six months. He walks independently with a broad-based gait and uses a stick outdoors. No recent falls. On examination today he rose from the chair without assistance. Tone is mildly increased in the right lower limb but range of movement is comfortable. There is a resolving callus under the right fifth metatarsal head; no ulceration; footwear advice reinforced.

Seizures: Bill and his key worker brought a diary for the past 6 months. Entries suggest brief episodes occurring intermittently, with months of quiet followed by isolated events. He describes occasional warning symptoms and short periods of unresponsiveness, recovering within minutes, without injury. Despite adherence to his medicines, attempts to increase doses have been curtailed by side effects (notably fatigue and cognitive slowing), and events continue to occur sporadically. The overall picture is of control constrained by tolerability, with residual breakthrough episodes every few months. There have been no ambulance call-outs or prolonged events.

Sleep and mood: He reports variable sleep but no sustained low mood. No alcohol use; continues to smoke fewer than five cigarettes daily and is open to cessation support. The assistance dog training team are satisfied with his engagement.

Investigations: Routine bloods from the GP in July 2025 showed normal electrolytes and liver profile; vitamin D adequate on supplementation. No recent drug levels requested.

Plan:  
- Maintain current antiepileptic regimen given prior side-effect burden when attempting higher doses.  
- Provide written seizure first aid and diary templates; encourage continued event logging.  
- Refer to smoking cessation service via practice team.  
- Podiatry review for recurrent forefoot callus; advise cushioned footwear.  
- Safety counselling reinforced, including bathing and cooking precautions; dog training team informed of guidance with Bill’s consent.  
- Check when next bloods are due in primary care; repeat U&E/LFT/Vit D within 6 months or sooner if clinical change.  
- Review in clinic in 6–9 months, sooner if events escalate or injuries occur.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: residual breakthrough episodes every few months

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly discusses seizures. It states that diary entries show months of quiet followed by isolated events and summarizes this as residual breakthrough episodes every few months. This implies roughly one isolated event per several months. There is no evidence of being seizure free for 6 months. Using the allowed vague unit, the best normalized label is 1 per multiple months.
seizure_frequency_number: residual breakthrough episodes every few months
reference: Tolerability-limited dosing with ongoing seizure events

flaws.description: Gold (1 per multiple months) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
